schools.summary: The opening of the practical half (Barnes); Paul beseeches (not commands), grounding the appeal in his bonds 'the prisoner of the Lord' (for the gospel's sake); to 'walk worthy of the vocation' — the life answering the calling, adorning the grace of chapters 1-3 with the graces of regenerate humanity (Ellicott).
Chrysostom: the teacher aims not at praise but at the salvation of his hearers.

The humble graces that knit believers together — 'lowliness' (humility, Phil 2:3, the first step to unity), 'meekness' (the gentle, unretaliating spirit), 'longsuffering' (patience bearing wrong over time), crowned by 'forbearing one another in love'; the virtues that absorb the daily offenses arising from human temper (Calvin).

'Endeavouring' (giving diligence) to 'keep the unity of the Spirit' — the oneness the Holy Spirit creates, to be guarded not manufactured; 'in the bond of peace' (peace the ligament, love the bond, Col 3:14); especially apt where Jew and Gentile met; believers keep, not create, the Spirit-wrought unity.

themes: keep the unity of the Spirit

schools.summary: The first triad of the sevenfold unity, terse and creed-like ('an embryo creed,' Ellicott) — 'one body' (the mystical body of Christ, all believers), 'one Spirit' (the Holy Spirit animating it), 'one hope' (the common heavenly glory); objective realities, not aspirations, so the church ought visibly to be one.

The central, Christ-focused triad — 'one Lord' (the Lord Jesus alone, the one Master, no rival), 'one faith' (the one trust in him and body of truth confessed, the same for Jew or Gentile), 'one baptism' (the single rite of initiation in the Trinity's name); from the calling to him who calls and the way of coming (Ellicott).

The climax — 'one God and Father of all,' the ultimate source of unity, completing the Trinity (Father, Son, Spirit across vv4-6); the threefold relation 'above all' (sovereign), 'through all' (pervading), 'in all' (indwelling); the main argument from which the rest flow, the Father gathering all to himself (Calvin).

themes: one God and Father of all

schools.summary: From unity to diversity — 'but' to every one grace is given (no believer giftless) 'according to the measure of the gift of Christ' (each the proportion Christ assigns, Rom 12:6); the diversity serving unity, for no member is complete in himself but depends on others (Calvin), the gifts binding the body in mutual service.

themes: the measure of the gift of Christ

schools.summary: A citation of Ps 68:18, a Messianic victory psalm — 'led captivity captive' (the conqueror leading vanquished foes; Christ triumphing over sin, death, Satan); 'gave gifts' the fruit of victory, the ascended Christ enriching his people with the spoils of his conquest.

debates.question: Why does Paul write 'gave gifts' where the Psalm reads 'received gifts'?

sides.position: the conqueror receives spoils in order to distribute them; Christ received the Spirit's fulness to bestow it — Paul draws out the meaning, not alters it

schools.summary: The word 'ascended' implies a prior 'descended' (one cannot go up who was not below); on Christ's deity, his coming down from heaven (saints ascend though never descended, Wesley); 'the lower parts of the earth' debated — the earth itself (incarnation, death, burial) or the regions beneath.
Most refer the descent to the depth of his self-emptying in incarnation and death, not a descent into the realm of the dead.

debates.question: What are 'the lower parts of the earth'?

sides.position: the earth itself (lower than heaven) — Christ's incarnation, humiliation, death, and burial
the regions beneath the earth, a descent into the realm of the dead

themes: the lower parts of the earth

schools.summary: One person, one purpose — 'he that descended is the same that ascended' (the Incarnation and Ascension of one Lord), now 'far above all heavens' (the supreme place, Eph 1:21); 'that he might fill all things' — the exalted Christ pervading the universe and his church with his presence and grace, the source of every gift.

The ascended Christ's gifts are the ministers themselves (Ellicott) — 'apostles' (foundational witnesses), 'prophets' (revelation), 'evangelists' (gospel pioneers), 'pastors and teachers' (settled shepherds); the first orders foundational/temporary, the latter perpetual; from the variety springs unity, as varied tones make one melody (Calvin).

debates.question: Are 'pastors and teachers' one office or two?

sides.position: a single class — the shepherd who also teaches

schools.summary: The threefold purpose — 'perfecting' (katartismos, equipping/mending, as setting a bone) the saints, 'for the work of the ministry/service,' 'for the edifying of the body'; the grammar suggesting the ministers equip the saints so the saints do the work and the body is built up — the whole church, not the clergy alone, sharing the work.

The goal toward which the ministry labours (continuing to the end, Calvin, embracing every individual) — 'the unity of the faith and knowledge of the Son of God,' 'a perfect man' (spiritual manhood/maturity), 'the measure of the stature of the fulness of Christ' (his complete likeness); both corporate and individual, transformation into the image of the Son.

themes: knowledge of the Son of God

schools.summary: The childhood maturity overcomes — 'no more children' (nepioi, the immature, undiscerning, some content to stay babes, Heb 5:13); 'tossed to and fro' (like waves) and 'carried about with every wind of doctrine,' the prey of 'the sleight of men' (trickery, the loaded dice of cheats, Wesley); immaturity making one easy prey for error.

The positive path — 'the truth' (sound doctrine, embraced and adhered to, the safeguard against being blown about) 'in love' (truth without love hardens, love without truth deceives; scolding profits not, Clarke); 'grow up into him in all things, the head, even Christ' — maturity as growth into Christ, not mere knowledge.

The body growing from its Head — 'from whom' (Christ the source of all life and increase, as sap from the root, Calvin); 'fitly joined together and compacted' (every member framed into place); 'by that which every joint supplieth' (each part's mutual contribution); 'increase... unto the edifying of itself in love' — growth from the Head yet through the members' loving ministry.

A solemn charge ('I say and testify in the Lord') to 'walk not as other Gentiles' — the converted no longer living like the unconverted; the root of pagan life named first, 'the vanity of their mind' (mataiotes, emptiness/futility of a mind that lost the knowledge of God, Rom 1:21), the inward source of the outward sins.

The dark inward roots — 'understanding darkened' (the mind blind in spiritual things), 'alienated from the life of God' (cut off from the divine life God lives and imparts), 'through the ignorance... because of the blindness of their heart' (a willful, moral ignorance from a hardened heart growing darker through sin, Clarke); separation from God's very life the heathen tragedy.

themes: alienated from the life of God

schools.summary: The depth of corruption — 'past feeling' (apelgekotes, conscience cauterized, the moral sense deadened; past feeling pain, which urges seeking a cure, Wesley); 'given themselves over' (willingly surrendered) 'unto lasciviousness' (unbridled lust) 'to work all uncleanness with greediness' (every impurity pursued insatiably, as a trade).

A sharp contrast — 'but ye have not so learned Christ'; the unique phrase 'learned Christ' (not merely about him, but Christ himself, as one learns a person — receiving his doctrine, Spirit, and self); incompatible with the old corruption, for one who has learned Christ 'cannot act thus' (Wesley); two masters, two natures.

'If so be that' a rhetorical doubt expressing strong affirmation ('since indeed'); three stages — 'heard him' (Christ preached and himself heard), 'taught by/in him' (instructed in union with Christ), 'as the truth is in Jesus' (the truth embodied in the historical Jesus); a faithful, thorough delivery, reproving superficial knowledge (Calvin).

themes: as the truth is in Jesus

schools.summary: The metaphor of clothing — 'put off' (apothesthai) the 'old man' (the corrupt Adamic nature, the body of sin of the 'former conversation') like a filthy worn-out garment; 'corrupt according to the deceitful lusts' (decaying under lusts that promise a happiness they cannot give, Wesley); the demand for repentance and mortification (Calvin).

The inward middle term — 'renewed' (ananeousthai, made new/young again, Col 3:10) 'in the spirit of your mind' (the inmost principle of understanding and will); the Spirit renovating all the faculties at their source, not curbing outward acts only; the hinge between putting off and putting on, for outward change without inward renewal is hollow.

themes: renewed in the spirit of your mind
the Spirit's work in the mind

schools.summary: The positive completion — 'put on the new man' (the brand-new nature of regeneration, worn in the life); 'after God is created' (by God, after his likeness, the image of God restored, a new creation, 2 Cor 5:17); 'in righteousness and true holiness' (the marks of the divine image — righteousness toward men, holiness toward God); the second creation by Christ's grace (Calvin).

The first duty of the new man — 'wherefore' (because the new man is put on) 'putting away lying, speak truth'; the reason 'we are members one of another' (as the body's members do not deceive one another, so Christ's body must deal truthfully); truth the first condition of the mutual confidence on which unity rests (Ellicott).

A permissive imperative, not a command to be angry — 'if ye be angry, sin not' (Ps 4:4); anger itself not necessarily sin (Christ felt it, Mark 3:5; God's anger burns against sin); anger at sin lawful but free from malice, directed at the fault while pitying the offender (Wesley); 'let not the sun go down' — reconcile quickly, lest emotion harden into resentment.

debates.question: Is 'be ye angry' a command or a concession?

sides.position: permissive — 'if ye are angry, sin not'; anger may be righteous but must not become sin

themes: let not the sun go down

schools.summary: 'Give place' (topon, room, a foothold) to the devil — connected to v26, nursed festering anger the very opening Satan seeks, turning a passing emotion into rooted bitterness; generally understood of Satan the tempter (not a human slanderer); every cherished sin gives the devil a foothold (Calvin), to be left no room.

themes: give no place to the devil

schools.summary: A threefold reversal — 'steal no more' (end the dishonest taking), 'rather let him labour... the thing which is good' (honest toil replacing idleness, the road back to stealing, Wesley; even unemployment no excuse, 2 Thess 3:10), 'that he may have to give to him that needeth' (the former thief now earning to give); vice replaced by its contrary virtue.

themes: that he may have to give

schools.summary: 'Corrupt' (sapros, rotten/putrid like decayed fruit, Matt 7:17) — foul, useless, harmful talk that defiles speaker and hearers (Henry); the high positive standard 'good to the use of edifying' (suited to the need) 'that it may minister grace' (conveying spiritual benefit); the test not the absence of evil only but the presence of good.

'Grieve not' (lypeite) the Holy Spirit — a person, not a mere influence, made sorrowful as a loving friend by unkind behaviour (Wesley); the foregoing and following sins summed up as grieving him (more than 'quenching' or 'resisting,' Ellicott); the precious motive 'whereby ye are sealed unto the day of redemption' (Eph 1:13) — the grieved Spirit the very One who secures salvation.

themes: sealed unto the day of redemption

schools.summary: A catalogue with a progression (Wesley) — 'bitterness' (settled sour resentment, the root), 'wrath' and 'anger' (heated passion and outbursts), 'clamour' (loud brawling), 'evil speaking' (slander/reviling), 'with all malice' (the underlying ill-will); 'be put away' (arthето, carried off like refuse, Col 3:8); the whole brood of angry tempers banished from the new man.

The graces replacing malice — 'kind' (chrestoi, used of God's own kindness, Rom 2:4), 'tenderhearted' (eusplanchnoi, compassionate, 1 Pet 3:8), 'forgiving one another' (the opposite of cherished malice); the pattern and motive 'even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you' — forgiveness measured by and flowing from God's free pardon, founded on what God has done in Christ (Chrysostom).

themes: as God for Christ's sake forgave you
